title: Composting difficult materials: cardboard, woody prunings, and citrus
subtitle: Which materials slow down, which materials are fine, and which to avoid entirely
excerpt: Most composting guidance produces a short approved list. The longer list of materials people actually have (corrugated cardboard, citrus peel, autumn leaves, woody prunings, paper) can all be composted with minor adjustments. The main variables are carbon-to-nitrogen ratio, particle size, and moisture.
sourceNotes: RHS composting guidance on what can and cannot be composted. WRAP guide to materials suitable for home composting. Charles Dowding notes on composting perennial weeds and woody material.

glossaryTerms.definition: The ratio of carbon to nitrogen in a compost input, written as C:N. Ideal compost feedstock blends reach approximately 25-30:1. High-carbon (brown) materials: cardboard (~400:1), straw (~80:1), autumn leaves (~60:1). High-nitrogen (green) materials: grass clippings (~20:1), food scraps (~15:1). Mixing browns and greens brings the blend into the optimal range.

content.text: Most composting problems come from too much of a single material rather than anything inherently wrong with the material itself. The key variables for slow-composting materials are: particle size (smaller decomposes faster), moisture content (dry materials need wetting), and the

Cardboard is an excellent brown material. Remove any plastic tape or glossy labels. Tear into pieces no larger than A5. Wet the cardboard before adding: dry cardboard repels water and sits inert in a heap for months. Soaked, torn cardboard decomposes in 4-8 weeks. Waxy or plastic-coated cardboard (some drinks cartons, waxed fruit boxes) does not break down in a home heap; avoid it.

Citrus peel breaks down fine in a well-balanced heap. The acidity and limonene content slow worm activity but do not harm the heap overall. Add in small quantities mixed with other materials. In a wormery, keep citrus to under 10% of additions by volume as worms avoid strongly acidic material.

Stems thicker than a pencil decompose very slowly whole. Chip or shred prunings before adding, or use them as a separate mulch layer rather than a compost input. Chipped wood at 25-50 mm pieces makes an excellent long-term mulch and eventually decomposes in place. If you add woody material to the compost heap, accept that it will take 12-18 months to break down fully.

Autumn leaves have a very high C:N ratio and decompose slowly in a main compost heap. The preferred approach is a separate leaf mould pile: a simple wire cage filled with leaves, kept moist, left for 12-24 months. The result is leaf mould, which is an excellent soil conditioner but has lower nutrients than finished compost. Oak and beech leaves take longer than soft leaves.
Meat, fish, and cooked food: attract rats and other pests in a garden heap. Use a sealed compost bin or council food waste collection instead.
Perennial weed roots (bindweed, couch grass, horsetail): survive most home compost heaps unless the heap reliably reaches 55°C throughout. Best to bin or dispose of separately.
Diseased plant material: blight-affected potatoes or tomatoes, club root brassicas. Do not add to a home heap; dispose of in household waste or council green waste.
Coal ash: contains toxic compounds. Wood ash from untreated wood is fine in small quantities.
